title: SQL vs NoSQL
description: A staff-level comparison of the relational model and the four NoSQL families, covering ACID vs BASE, access-pattern-first modeling, consistency and CAP positioning of real engines, and how to actually choose a data store rather than chase a label.

blocks.text: "SQL vs NoSQL" is the wrong framing made famous by a marketing era. SQL is a query language; NoSQL is a grab-bag of storage models that mostly share one trait — they do not use the relational model the same way. The real decision a staff engineer makes is not 'relational or not' but 'which data model, which consistency guarantees, and which scaling story fit my access patterns and my failure budget.' Answering that well requires knowing what each engine actually does under load, not which logo is trendy.

This topic contrasts the relational model with the four NoSQL families — document, key-value, wide-column, and graph — through the lenses that matter in production: ACID vs BASE, normalization vs denormalization, schema-on-write vs schema-on-read, vertical vs horizontal scaling and sharding, strong vs eventual consistency, query power, secondary indexes, and cross-document transactions. It positions concrete engines (PostgreSQL, MongoDB, DynamoDB, Cassandra, Redis, Neo4j) on the CAP map and ends with a decision procedure.

Two myths are dismantled along the way: that 'NoSQL means schemaless' (it means schema-on-read — the schema still exists, it just lives in your code) and that 'SQL doesn't scale' (PostgreSQL and MySQL run some of the largest workloads on earth; what's hard is multi-master writes, which NoSQL also doesn't get for free).

The data store is the decision you are most stuck with. Application code is rewritten constantly; a 50TB store with five years of production data, downstream consumers, and replication topology is a migration project measured in quarters. Choosing on hype rather than access patterns means either re-platforming later or building expensive workarounds — joining documents in application code, bolting a search engine onto a key-value store, or running distributed transactions a system was never designed for.

items: Correctness: ACID vs BASE decides whether two concurrent writers can silently overwrite each other or read stale data, and whether you can move money in a single atomic step.
Scale: the write throughput ceiling of a single primary vs a sharded ring is the difference between vertical-scaling headroom and a horizontal architecture you must design for from day one.
Latency and cost: a query that is a single-partition lookup in one model is a cross-partition scatter-gather in another — same logical result, 10-100x the cost.
Flexibility vs guarantees: schema-on-read buys agility and pays for it with implicit, drifting contracts that surface as runtime bugs instead of migration errors.

blocks.text: Most 'we picked the wrong database' postmortems are actually 'we modeled for the wrong access patterns.' The engine rarely fails you; the data model you imposed on it does. Model the queries first, then pick the store that serves them cheaply.

The relational model stores data as tables (relations) of rows and columns, with a fixed schema enforced on write. Its superpower is the declarative join: you store each fact once, normalized, and reassemble any combination at query time. You do not have to know your access patterns in advance — the query planner figures out how to satisfy whatever ad-hoc query you write, using indexes, joins, aggregation, and subqueries. This generality is exactly what makes it the safe default for transactional systems and unknown future questions.

blocks.code:
-- Schema-on-write: structure and constraints enforced by the engine
CREATE TABLE customers (
  id     BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  email  CITEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  name   TEXT NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE orders (
  id           BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  customer_id  BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES customers(id),
  status       TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (status IN ('pending','paid','shipped','cancelled')),
  total_cents  BIGINT NOT NULL CHECK (total_cents >= 0),
  created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE order_items (
  order_id   BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES orders(id),
  product_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
  qty        INT NOT NULL CHECK (qty > 0),
  PRIMARY KEY (order_id, product_id)
);

-- Ad-hoc join the engine plans for you: top 5 customers by spend last month
SELECT c.name, SUM(o.total_cents) AS spend_cents
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
WHERE o.status = 'paid'
  AND o.created_at >= date_trunc('month', now()) - interval '1 month'
GROUP BY c.id, c.name
ORDER BY spend_cents DESC
LIMIT 5;

blocks.text: The trade-off is that joins are work, and at very high scale on commodity-sized partitions, joining across shards is expensive or impossible. Relational engines also historically scaled writes vertically — one primary accepts all writes — though that ceiling is far higher than folklore admits and modern distributed SQL (CockroachDB, Spanner, Vitess) pushes it further.

PostgreSQL is not 'just' relational: it ships JSONB with GIN indexes, arrays, full-text search, and partitioning. A document column inside a relational table often beats a separate document database for moderate scale, because you keep transactions and joins while gaining flexible nested data.
"NoSQL" is not one thing. It is four distinct data models that solve different problems. Conflating them is the source of most bad advice.

items: Document (MongoDB, Couchbase): stores self-contained JSON/BSON documents keyed by id. Rich nested structure, secondary indexes, per-document atomicity. Great when data is naturally hierarchical and accessed as an aggregate.

Key-value (Redis, DynamoDB at its core, Riak): the simplest model — opaque value behind a key, O(1) get/put. Blazing fast, trivially shardable, but you can only query by key (plus whatever secondary indexes the engine bolts on).

Wide-column (Cassandra, HBase, ScyllaDB): rows partitioned by a partition key, with clustering columns sorting within a partition. Tuned for massive write throughput and time-series / event data with a known query shape.

Graph (Neo4j, Neptune): nodes and edges as first-class citizens with index-free adjacency, so multi-hop traversals (friends-of-friends, fraud rings, recommendations) are cheap where SQL recursive joins are not.

rows: Querying by anything but the key

blocks.text: Picking 'a NoSQL database' is meaningless. A document store and a wide-column store are as different from each other as either is from PostgreSQL. Always name the family and the engine, because their consistency, query, and scaling characteristics barely overlap.

A document store keeps an aggregate together: an order and its line items live in one document, so the common read is a single fetch with no join. You denormalize deliberately — embedding data that is read together and changes together. The cost is that the same fact (a product name, a price) may be duplicated across many documents, and updating it means a multi-document write that is not atomic by default.

Reads are now one round trip. Queries use secondary indexes and the aggregation pipeline rather than joins. MongoDB does support multi-document ACID transactions since 4.0, but they are a deliberate escape hatch, not the default mode — and they cost more than the single-document atomic writes the model is optimized for.

Embed what is read together and bounded; reference what is large, unbounded, or shared and frequently updated. An order's line items embed; a customer's full order history does not — it would grow without limit and blow past the 16MB document cap.

Wide-column and key-value engines force you to model access-pattern-first. There is no query planner improvising joins; you design the partition key so that each query reads one partition, and the clustering/sort key so that rows arrive pre-sorted. DynamoDB single-table design takes this to its conclusion: heterogeneous item types share one table, packed so that an entire access pattern resolves in a single Query on one partition.

Cassandra (CQL looks like SQL but is not relational) is the wide-column workhorse for write-heavy, time-series, and event workloads. You define the primary key as (partition key, clustering columns) and you create one table per query — the same data is written to several tables, denormalized, because you cannot join. The reward is linear, leaderless horizontal scaling and tunable consistency per request.

blocks.code:
-- Cassandra CQL: a table designed for ONE query (events by device, recent first)
CREATE TABLE events_by_device (
  device_id   text,
  bucket      text,        -- e.g. '2026-06' to cap partition size
  occurred_at timestamp,
  event_type  text,
  payload     text,
  PRIMARY KEY ((device_id, bucket), occurred_at)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (occurred_at DESC);

-- Single-partition read, the only shape this table serves efficiently:
SELECT * FROM events_by_device
WHERE device_id = 'dev_7' AND bucket = '2026-06'
LIMIT 100;

blocks.text: Choosing a partition key is the single most important decision in DynamoDB and Cassandra. A low-cardinality or skewed key creates a hot partition that throttles throughput while the rest of the cluster idles. Model for even distribution and bounded partition size before you write a line of code.

ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) is the transactional contract relational engines were built around: a transaction either fully happens or not at all, leaves the database in a valid state, runs as if alone, and survives a crash once committed. BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent) is the philosophy many distributed NoSQL systems adopt to stay available under partition: accept temporary inconsistency, and converge later.

rows: Eventual; readers may see stale state
May reject writes to stay correct
Money, inventory, anything that must balance

blocks.text: The dividing line is not 'SQL has ACID and NoSQL doesn't.' MongoDB and DynamoDB both offer ACID transactions; many distributed SQL systems relax isolation under load. The honest statement is: ACID is cheap on a single node and expensive across many nodes, so systems built for horizontal scale tend to default to weaker guarantees and let you pay for stronger ones per operation.

DynamoDB offers strongly consistent reads (opt-in, costs more read capacity) and ACID TransactWriteItems across up to 100 items. Cassandra offers tunable consistency (ONE, QUORUM, ALL) and lightweight transactions via Paxos. 'Eventual' is a default, not a hard limit — you can buy strength where you need it.

CAP says that during a network partition a distributed system must choose between consistency (every read sees the latest write) and availability (every request gets a non-error response). It is a statement about behavior under partition, not a permanent three-way trade — when the network is healthy you get both. Eventual consistency means replicas may temporarily disagree, producing real anomalies: read-your-own-writes failures, stale reads, and lost updates without conflict resolution.

rows: Sync replicas can block writes to stay consistent
Multi-AZ; strong reads cost more capacity
QUORUM R+W > N gives strong consistency
Redis Cluster trades some consistency for HA

blocks.text: The practical lever in Dynamo-style systems is the quorum formula: if reads (R) plus writes (W) exceed the replication factor (N), a read is guaranteed to see the latest write — R + W > N gives strong consistency at the cost of latency and availability. Cassandra exposes this directly; you tune it per query rather than for the whole database.

Eventual consistency is not 'usually fine.' A 'like' count converging is fine; a bank balance, an inventory decrement, or a unique-username check is not. Map each operation to the weakest consistency it can tolerate, and never let the convenient default decide for the operations that move money or enforce uniqueness.

Vertical scaling means a bigger machine; horizontal scaling means more machines. Reads scale horizontally in almost any system via replicas. Writes are the hard part: a single-primary relational database scales writes vertically until the box runs out, then needs application-level sharding or a distributed SQL engine. NoSQL systems built for scale (DynamoDB, Cassandra) shard writes automatically by partition key — that automatic horizontal write scaling, not the data model, is their real differentiator.

items: Replication: copies of the data for read scale and failover. Async replicas lag (stale reads); sync replicas are consistent but slower and can block on partition.
Sharding/partitioning: splitting data across nodes by a key. Hash sharding spreads load evenly but kills range queries; range sharding keeps ranges together but risks hotspots.
Partition key choice: determines distribution and which queries stay single-node. The wrong key creates hot partitions and forces cross-shard scatter-gather.
Cross-shard operations: joins and transactions that span shards are slow or unavailable — which is precisely why access-pattern-first models avoid them.

blocks.text: 'SQL doesn't scale' is folklore. A single PostgreSQL primary on modern hardware handles tens of thousands of write TPS and tens of terabytes. What is genuinely hard in any system is multi-master writes with strong consistency — and NoSQL does not give you that for free either; it gives you horizontal writes by relaxing consistency or constraining the query model.

Schema-on-write (relational) validates structure and constraints at insert time: a bad row is rejected at the door, and every reader can trust the shape. Schema-on-read (most NoSQL) accepts whatever you write and interprets structure when you read it. The schema does not disappear — it moves from the database into your application code, where it is implicit, versioned by deploys, and enforced only as carefully as your code enforces it.

Schema-on-read is a real benefit for genuinely heterogeneous or fast-evolving data, and for ingesting data whose shape you do not yet know. The mistake is treating it as freedom from schema design rather than a deferral of schema enforcement to a place with weaker guarantees.

The most expensive misconception in this area is that NoSQL frees you from data modeling. The opposite is true: relational modeling is forgiving because the planner can join its way out of a mediocre schema, whereas DynamoDB and Cassandra punish a bad model immediately with hot partitions, full-table scans, or queries you simply cannot express. NoSQL demands more upfront modeling discipline, not less.

items: Myth: 'No schema, so I can move fast.' Reality: the schema lives in your code and in every consumer; drift becomes runtime bugs instead of caught migrations.

Myth: 'SQL can't handle our scale.' Reality: read replicas, partitioning, and connection pooling take a single primary very far; most teams hit their own modeling and N+1 problems long before the engine's ceiling.
Myth: 'NoSQL is faster.' Reality: it is faster for the access pattern it was modeled for. For anything else it is slower or impossible, because there is no planner to improvise.
Myth: 'We'll figure out queries later.' Reality: in access-pattern-first stores, an unanticipated query may require a full data migration to a new table or index.

blocks.text: If your justification for NoSQL is 'we don't want to think about schema yet,' you have chosen the family that punishes that mindset hardest. Either model your access patterns rigorously, or stay relational where ad-hoc queries are cheap and schema is enforced for you.

sections.heading: Query Power, Joins, and Secondary Indexes

blocks.text: Relational engines give you the full algebra — joins, subqueries, window functions, ad-hoc aggregation — planned and optimized for you. NoSQL trades that generality for predictable performance at scale. Document stores offer secondary indexes and aggregation pipelines but no efficient cross-document joins. Wide-column stores serve essentially one query shape per table. Key-value stores query by key alone. Graph stores invert the trade: relationship traversal is cheap, bulk analytic scans are not.

items: Joins vs denormalization: SQL joins at read time keeping one copy of each fact; NoSQL denormalizes at write time, duplicating facts so reads need no join — you trade read simplicity for write fan-out and sync responsibility.

Secondary indexes: in DynamoDB (GSI/LSI) and Cassandra they are more constrained and costly than relational indexes — a GSI is a separate replicated table, and Cassandra secondary indexes can fan out across the whole ring.
Single-table design: DynamoDB collapses multiple entity types into one table so an access pattern resolves in a single query, eliminating joins by construction.

When a join wins: for low-to-moderate scale with many ad-hoc or evolving queries, a relational join is simpler, cheaper to maintain, and avoids the consistency burden of keeping denormalized copies in sync.

blocks.text: A join beats denormalization whenever the duplicated data changes often, must stay consistent, or whenever the set of queries is open-ended. Denormalization beats joins when reads vastly outnumber writes, the access pattern is fixed, and the data is read together as an aggregate.

sections.heading: Polyglot Persistence and How to Choose

blocks.text: Mature systems are rarely single-store. Polyglot persistence means using the right engine per workload behind clear service boundaries: PostgreSQL for transactional core, Redis for cache and sessions, Elasticsearch for search, a wide-column store for the firehose of events, a graph database for relationship queries. The cost is operational complexity and keeping stores in sync (usually via change-data-capture), so add a second store only when one store genuinely cannot serve a workload, not by default.

items: Start relational. PostgreSQL is the highest-leverage default: transactions, joins, JSONB for flexibility, and it scales further than most teams will ever need.
Reach for document (MongoDB) when data is a self-contained, hierarchical aggregate read and written as a unit, and the schema evolves fast.
Reach for key-value (Redis, DynamoDB) for cache, sessions, rate limits, and simple high-throughput key lookups with predictable patterns.
Reach for wide-column (Cassandra) for write-heavy, time-series, append-only data at horizontal scale with a fixed query shape.
Reach for graph (Neo4j) when the value is in multi-hop relationships — recommendations, fraud rings, social graphs, dependency trees.
Let consistency requirements veto the choice: anything that must be atomic and correct (money, inventory, uniqueness) belongs in a store with strong guarantees for that operation.

blocks.text: Decision procedure: (1) enumerate your access patterns and write/read ratios; (2) determine the consistency each operation truly needs; (3) estimate scale and growth; (4) pick the simplest store that serves the patterns with the required guarantees — usually relational. Add specialized stores only for workloads that one demonstrably cannot serve.

Beyond the data model, the day-two cost differs sharply. Managed services (DynamoDB, Aurora, Atlas) trade money for removing backups, failover, scaling, and patching from your plate. Self-managed Cassandra or a sharded Postgres fleet is powerful but demands real operational expertise — repair, compaction, rebalancing, and topology changes are not free.

items: Backups and recovery: point-in-time recovery is mature in relational and managed NoSQL; verify it exists and is tested before you depend on it.
Migrations: schema-on-write migrations are explicit and reviewable; schema-on-read 'migrations' are background backfills your code must tolerate mid-flight.
Cost model: DynamoDB bills per request and storage — cheap at low scale, surprising under heavy scans; provisioned databases bill for the box whether busy or idle.
Observability: know how to read a relational EXPLAIN plan, a Mongo explain(), and DynamoDB consumed-capacity metrics; the failure modes are store-specific.

blocks.text: Factor the team into the decision. The best-fit engine your team cannot operate well at 3am is worse than the good-enough engine they know cold. Operational maturity is a first-class selection criterion, not an afterthought.

diagrams.title: Relational Model: Orders ER Diagram
Decision Tree: Choosing a Data Store
CAP Positioning of Real Engines
diagrams.content:
Consistency (C)
                       |
   PostgreSQL (CP) -----+----- MongoDB majority (CP)
   Neo4j cluster (CP)   |       DynamoDB strong (CP)
                        |
   ---------------------+---------------------  Partition
                        |    tolerance (P)
   Redis single (CA*)   |
                        |
                 Cassandra QUORUM tunable
                 default Cassandra ---> Availability (AP)
                 DynamoDB eventual

  * Redis single node is CA only because it is not partitioned;
    Redis Cluster shifts toward AP under network splits.
  Under a partition every distributed engine must give up C or A;
  the labels show which side each one leans toward by default.

questions.question: What are the four main families of NoSQL databases and how do they differ?
questions.answer: The four families are document (MongoDB), key-value (Redis, DynamoDB core), wide-column (Cassandra), and graph (Neo4j). Document stores keep self-contained JSON aggregates queried by key plus secondary indexes. Key-value stores map an opaque value to a key with O(1) access but no querying beyond the key. Wide-column stores partition rows by a partition key with clustering columns for sort order, tuned for write-heavy and time-series workloads. Graph stores make nodes and edges first-class so multi-hop traversals are cheap. They are as different from each other as any of them is from a relational database, so naming the family matters far more than the 'NoSQL' label.
followUps: Which family fits a session cache?
Which fits a social graph?

questions.question: Does 'NoSQL' mean schemaless?
questions.answer: No. NoSQL stores use schema-on-read rather than schema-on-write: the database accepts whatever you write and the structure is interpreted at read time. The schema does not vanish — it moves from the engine into your application code, where it is implicit, versioned by deploys, and enforced only as carefully as your code enforces it. This buys agility for heterogeneous or fast-evolving data, but it converts what would have been caught migrations into runtime bugs when documents drift across shapes. Access-pattern-first stores like DynamoDB and Cassandra actually demand more rigorous upfront modeling than relational databases, not less, because there is no query planner to improvise around a poor schema.
followUps: How do you reintroduce schema enforcement in MongoDB?
What is schema drift?

questions.question: Is it true that 'SQL doesn't scale'?
questions.answer: That is folklore. A single PostgreSQL or MySQL primary on modern hardware handles tens of thousands of write transactions per second and tens of terabytes, and reads scale horizontally with replicas and partitioning. What is genuinely hard is multi-master writes with strong consistency, and NoSQL does not solve that for free either — it achieves horizontal write scale by relaxing consistency (eventual) or constraining the query model (single-partition access). Most teams hit their own N+1 patterns, missing indexes, and modeling mistakes long before they reach the engine's real ceiling. Distributed SQL engines like CockroachDB, Spanner, and Vitess push the write ceiling further while keeping SQL.
followUps: What actually limits single-primary write scale?
What is Vitess?

questions.question: What is the difference between ACID and BASE?
questions.answer: ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) is the transactional contract of relational engines: transactions are all-or-nothing, leave valid state, run as if alone, and survive crashes once committed. BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent) is the philosophy of many distributed systems: stay available under partition, accept temporary inconsistency, and converge later. The dividing line is not SQL versus NoSQL — MongoDB and DynamoDB both offer ACID transactions, and distributed SQL sometimes relaxes isolation. The honest framing is that ACID is cheap on one node and expensive across many, so horizontally scaled systems default to weaker guarantees and let you pay for stronger ones per operation.
followUps: Can a NoSQL store be ACID?
What does eventual consistency cost you?

questions.question: When does a join beat denormalization?
questions.answer: A join beats denormalization when the duplicated data changes often, must stay consistent, or when the set of queries is open-ended. With a join you keep exactly one copy of each fact and the engine reassembles it at read time, so an update touches one place and any future query is expressible. Denormalization wins the opposite case: reads vastly outnumber writes, the access pattern is fixed, and the data is naturally read together as an aggregate, so paying once at write time to avoid a join on every read is worth it. The hidden cost of denormalization is that you now own keeping every duplicate in sync, and that sync logic is a frequent source of subtle corruption. For low-to-moderate scale with evolving queries, the relational join is usually simpler and safer.
followUps: How do you keep denormalized copies in sync?
What is read amplification?

questions.question: What real anomalies does eventual consistency produce?
questions.answer: Eventual consistency lets replicas temporarily disagree, which surfaces as concrete bugs. Read-your-own-writes failures: a user updates their profile and then sees the old value because their read hit a lagging replica. Stale reads: a dashboard shows numbers from seconds ago. Lost updates: two concurrent writers each read, modify, and write back, and one overwrites the other unless you use conditional writes or CRDTs. Monotonic-read violations: a user sees a value, refreshes, and sees an older value as they bounce between replicas. The fix is to require stronger guarantees only where needed — quorum reads/writes (R + W > N), read-your-writes session consistency, or conditional updates — rather than treating eventual consistency as universally acceptable.
followUps: How does R + W > N give strong consistency?
What is a CRDT?

questions.question: Why does DynamoDB favor single-table design?
questions.answer: DynamoDB has no joins and bills per request, so the goal is to resolve each access pattern in a single Query against one partition. Single-table design packs multiple entity types into one table using composite keys (a partition key that groups an aggregate and a sort key that orders and types items within it), so fetching a customer and all their orders is one request instead of several. It feels alien to relational thinkers because it deliberately denormalizes and co-locates heterogeneous items, but it is the natural conclusion of access-pattern-first modeling: you design the keys around the queries you must serve. The trade-off is rigidity — a new, unanticipated access pattern may require a new global secondary index or a data migration, which is why you enumerate access patterns exhaustively before designing the keys.
followUps: What is a GSI and how does it differ from an LSI?
How do you handle a new access pattern later?

questions.question: What does the CAP theorem actually say, and how do PostgreSQL and Cassandra position on it?
questions.answer: CAP says that during a network partition a distributed system must choose between consistency (every read sees the latest write) and availability (every request gets a non-error response). It is a statement about behavior under partition, not a permanent trade — with a healthy network you get both. PostgreSQL with synchronous replication leans CP: it may block or reject writes rather than serve inconsistent data. Cassandra defaults toward AP: it stays writable during a partition and reconciles later, though QUORUM reads and writes where R + W > N let you opt into strong consistency. The lever in Dynamo-style systems is the quorum formula, tuned per request, trading latency and availability for consistency.
followUps: What is PACELC?
When would you choose AP over CP?

questions.question: Why is the partition key the most important choice in DynamoDB or Cassandra?
questions.answer: The partition key determines how data is distributed across nodes and which queries stay on a single node. A good key has high cardinality and even access, so load spreads uniformly and each query reads one partition. A low-cardinality or skewed key creates a hot partition: one node is throttled and overloaded while the rest of the cluster idles, capping your effective throughput far below the provisioned total. It also fixes your query model — you can only query efficiently by the partition key (plus clustering or sort key), so a key that does not match your access patterns forces expensive scans or cross-partition scatter-gather. You also must bound partition size, often by adding a time bucket to the key, to avoid unbounded growth.
followUps: What is a write-sharding technique for hot keys?
How do you bound a Cassandra partition?

questions.question: What is polyglot persistence and when is it worth the complexity?
questions.answer: Polyglot persistence means using different data stores for different workloads behind clear service boundaries — for example PostgreSQL for the transactional core, Redis for cache and sessions, Elasticsearch for search, and a wide-column store for an event firehose. It is worth it when one store genuinely cannot serve a workload well: full-text search, multi-hop graph traversal, or a write firehose that overwhelms a single primary. The cost is real: more systems to operate, back up, and monitor, plus the burden of keeping stores in sync, usually via change-data-capture. The right discipline is to start with one store (usually relational) and add a specialized one only for a workload it demonstrably cannot handle, never by default.
followUps: How do you keep two stores in sync?
What is change-data-capture?

questions.question: Can MongoDB do multi-document transactions, and should you rely on them?
questions.answer: Yes, MongoDB supports multi-document ACID transactions since version 4.0 (and across sharded clusters since 4.2). But they are an escape hatch, not the default operating mode. The document model is optimized for single-document atomic writes, so the idiomatic approach is to embed data that must change together into one document and get atomicity for free. Multi-document transactions cost more in latency and resource contention, hold locks longer, and partially undercut the scaling story that motivates a document store. If you find yourself needing them frequently, it is a signal your data should either be re-modeled as aggregates or live in a relational database where multi-row transactions are the cheap, native path.
followUps: How does embedding give you atomicity for free?
When is frequent multi-doc transaction a smell?

questions.question: What is Redis good and bad at?
questions.answer: Redis is an in-memory key-value store, so it is exceptional at low-latency, high-throughput access by key: caching, session storage, rate limiting, leaderboards (sorted sets), pub/sub, and distributed locks. Its rich data structures (strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams) make it far more than a plain cache. It is weak as a primary system of record for large datasets because data lives in RAM, which is expensive, and durability is a trade-off — RDB snapshots and AOF logs reduce but do not eliminate the risk of losing recent writes on a crash. It also cannot serve ad-hoc queries beyond its key and structure operations. Use Redis to accelerate and offload, not as the durable source of truth for business-critical relational data.
followUps: How does Redis handle durability?
When does Redis Cluster weaken consistency?

questions.question: When should you reach for a graph database like Neo4j?
questions.answer: Reach for a graph database when the value of your data is in the relationships and you traverse many hops: friends-of-friends, recommendation paths, fraud rings, supply-chain dependencies, or access-control hierarchies. Graph engines use index-free adjacency, meaning each node directly references its neighbors, so a multi-hop traversal is O(hops) rather than the cascade of expensive joins or recursive CTEs a relational engine needs for the same query. The trade-off is that graph databases are weaker at bulk scans and aggregate analytics, and sharding a large, densely connected graph is genuinely hard because edges cross partitions. If your queries are mostly tabular with the occasional one-or-two-hop relationship, a relational database with a recursive query is simpler; the graph store pays off when deep traversal is the core workload.
followUps: What is index-free adjacency?
Why is sharding a graph hard?

questions.question: How do you actually choose between SQL and NoSQL for a new service?
questions.answer: Drive the decision from access patterns, consistency needs, and scale — not from the label. First enumerate the queries and the read/write ratio. Second, determine the weakest consistency each operation can tolerate; anything touching money, inventory, or uniqueness needs strong guarantees. Third, estimate scale and growth honestly. Fourth, pick the simplest store that serves those patterns with the required guarantees, which is usually PostgreSQL because it offers transactions, joins, JSONB flexibility, and more headroom than most teams need. Choose a specialized store only when a workload demonstrably outgrows it: document for self-contained aggregates, key-value for cache and hot lookups, wide-column for write-heavy time-series, graph for deep traversals. Finally, weigh whether your team can operate the chosen engine well, because operability is a first-class criterion.
followUps: What questions reveal the access patterns?
Why is PostgreSQL the safe default?

challenges.title: Model E-Commerce Orders in Normalized SQL and DynamoDB Single-Table
challenges.prompt: An e-commerce service must serve these access patterns: (1) fetch a customer with all their orders, newest first; (2) fetch a single order with its line items; (3) list all 'paid' orders across customers for a fulfillment dashboard. Design (a) a normalized relational schema with the queries, and (b) a DynamoDB single-table design with the key structure and one secondary index that serves all three patterns. Explain the trade-off between the two.
hints: In SQL, model customers, orders, and order_items as separate tables joined by foreign keys; index orders by (customer_id, created_at) for pattern 1.
In DynamoDB, use a partition key that groups an aggregate (CUST#id) and a sort key that orders and types items (ORDER#date#id, ITEM#...).
Pattern 3 crosses all customers, so it needs a Global Secondary Index keyed by status — the base table cannot serve it from a single partition.
challenges.explanation: The relational design stores each fact once and serves all three patterns with indexes and a join, and it would serve a fourth, unanticipated query without schema change because the planner improvises. Pattern 3 needs its own index on status, but adding it is a one-line online operation. The DynamoDB design instead encodes the access patterns directly into the keys: pattern 1 is a single Query on the customer partition filtered by the ORDER# sort-key prefix, pattern 2 is a single Query on the order partition that returns the header and item rows together, and pattern 3 cannot come from the base table because it spans all customers, so it requires a Global Secondary Index keyed by status. The DynamoDB version is faster and horizontally scalable for exactly these three patterns, but rigid: a genuinely new access pattern may demand a new GSI or a data migration, whereas the relational schema absorbs new queries for free. The core trade-off is generality and consistency (relational) versus predictable single-partition performance at scale (DynamoDB), and the right answer depends on whether your access patterns are fixed and your scale demands horizontal writes.

challenges.title: Translate a Relational Aggregation to a MongoDB Pipeline
challenges.prompt: A relational reporting query returns the top 3 product categories by total revenue from paid orders in the last 30 days, joining orders and order_items. Translate it into a MongoDB aggregation pipeline assuming an 'orders' collection where each document embeds its items array with category and lineTotalCents fields. Show both the SQL and the pipeline, and note what changes because the data is embedded rather than joined.
hints: The SQL needs a JOIN between orders and order_items; in the document model the items are already embedded, so you $unwind the array instead of joining.
Use $match to filter status and date first so the pipeline scans fewer documents, then $unwind, then $group by category.
Finish with $sort on the summed revenue descending and $limit 3, mirroring ORDER BY ... LIMIT.
challenges.explanation: The relational query joins two tables because line items live in their own normalized table referencing the order. In the document model the items are embedded inside each order, so there is no join to perform; instead $unwind flattens the embedded array into one virtual document per line item, after which $group, $sort, and $limit mirror the SQL GROUP BY, ORDER BY, and LIMIT exactly. Putting $match first is important: it filters orders by status and date before the more expensive $unwind and $group stages run, and the supporting compound index on status and createdAt keeps that stage from scanning the whole collection. The embedded model makes per-order reads a single round trip and avoids the join entirely, which is the document store's strength, but it also means category is duplicated across every line item in every order, so a category rename would require touching many documents rather than one row. This is the normalization-versus-denormalization trade-off made concrete: the relational version keeps one copy and joins at read time, while the document version copies for read locality and pays at write time and in aggregation complexity.

references.label: PostgreSQL Documentation: SQL Queries and Joins
Alex DeBrie: The DynamoDB Single-Table Design Guide
AWS DynamoDB Developer Guide: Best Practices for Design
